content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    : File §§ 82005, 82034, 87207                    Date : April 3, 1979

                                                         79-007

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Lee Rosenthal

Subject:

        Tony Alperin called with the following question:  A filer has
partnership interests in a number of race horses.  Each horse
is owned by the filer and a number of different people i.e., Horse #1
is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by A, and 1/3 by B.  Horse #2 is owned 1/3
by Filer, 1/3 by X and 1/3 by Y.  How should the filer report his
ownership interests and income from the horses?

        I told Tony to treat each horse as a separate business entity
for purposes of reporting investments and income.  Under this approach
his interest in Horse #1 is reported as an investment in the #1
partnership, his interest in Horse # 2 reported as an investment in
Horse #2 partnership, etc.  Income would be treated similarly i.e.,
the filer would report income from Horse #1 partnership and income
from customers of Horse #1 partnership; income from Horse #2 partner-
ship and income from customers of Horse #2 partnership.

        Tony suggested another approach which would be to treat the
interests in the horses as personal property and not as investments
and require reporting of income as received directly and was
received through a business entity, i.e., income from sale of the
horse, stud fees or purses.  This approach has the advantage of
requiring disclosure of horse sources of income at a §250 level (as
required by Section 87207(a)) rather than the §10,000 level specified
by Section 87207(b) for income of business entities.  However, I
believe that the horse owning partnerships are most analogous to
business entities and ought to be treated as business entities.

LR:plh

sections.question: A filer has partnership interests in a number of race horses. Each horse is owned by the filer and a number of different people i.e., Horse #1 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by A and 1/3 by B, Horse #2 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by X and 1/3 by Y. How should the filer report his ownership interests and income from the horses?
sections.conclusion: I told Tony to treat each horse as a separate business entity for purposes of reporting investments and income. Under this approach his interest in Horse #1 is reported as an investment in Horse #1 partnership, his interest in Horse #2 reported as an investment in Horse #2 partnership, etc. Income would be treated similarly i.e., the filer would report income from Horse #1 partnership and income from customers of Horse #1 partnership; income from Horse #2 partnership and income from customers of Horse #2 partnership. However, I believe that the horse owning partnerships are most analogous to business entities and ought to be treated as business entities.
sections.question_synthetic: How should a filer report partnership interests in multiple race horses and income derived from those horses under FPPC reporting requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that each horse partnership should be treated as a separate business entity for reporting purposes, with each partnership interest reported as an investment and income reported through the business entity structure rather than as direct personal property income.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memorandum format documenting oral advice given by Lee Rosenthal to Tony Alperin regarding horse partnership disclosure; statutory references to Sections 82005, 82034, and 87207; alternative approach mentioned but rejected.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A filer has partnership interests in a number of race horses. Each horse is owned by the filer and a number of different people i.e., Horse #1 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by A and 1/3 by B, Horse #2 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by X and 1/3 by Y. How should the filer report his ownership interests and income from the horses?

CONCLUSION: I told Tony to treat each horse as a separate business entity for purposes of reporting investments and income. Under this approach his interest in Horse #1 is reported as an investment in Horse #1 partnership, his interest in Horse #2 reported as an investment in Horse #2 partnership, etc. Income would be treated similarly i.e., the filer would report income from Horse #1 partnership and income from customers of Horse #1 partnership; income from Horse #2 partnership and income from customers of Horse #2 partnership. However, I believe that the horse owning partnerships are most analogous to business entities and ought to be treated as business entities.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To: File §§ 82005, 82034, 87207 Date: April 3, 1979 From: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Lee Rosenthal Subject: Tony Alperin called with the following question: A filer has partnership interests in a number of race horses. Each horse is owned by the filer and a number of different people i.e., Horse #1 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by A and 1/3 by B, Horse #2 is owned 1/3 by Filer, 1/3 by X and 1/3 by Y. How should the filer report his ownership interests and income from the horses? I told Tony to treat each horse as a separate business entity for purposes of reporting investments and income. Under this approach his interest in Horse #1 is reported as an investment in Horse #1 partnership, his interest in Horse #2 reported as an investment in Horse #2 partnership, etc. Income would be treated similarly i.e., the filer would report income from Horse #1 partnership and income from customers of Horse #1 partnership; income from Horse #2 partnership and income from customers of Horse #2 partnership. Tony suggested another approach which would be to treat the interests in the horses as personal property and not as investments and require reporting of income as received directly and not as received through a business entity, i.e., income from sale of the horse, stud fees or purses. This approach has the advantage of requiring disclosure of horse sources of income at a $250 level (as required by Section 87207(a)) rather than the $10,000 level specified by Section 87207(b) for income of business entities. However, I believe that the horse owning partnerships are most analogous to business entities and ought to be treated as business entities. LR:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC memorandum advising on how a filer should report partnership interests in multiple race horses and related income, concluding that each horse partnership should be treated as a separate business entity for reporting purposes.
